source.agency_name: Texas Office of the Attorney General — Child Support Division

steps: Determine gross monthly income (wages, self-employment, and other resources).
Subtract Social Security taxes, federal income tax (computed as a single filer claiming one exemption), union dues, and health/dental insurance ordered for the child to get net monthly resources.

If net resources are below $1,000/month, apply the low-income schedule (15%/20%/25%/30%/35% for 1-5 children). Otherwise apply the standard schedule (20%/25%/30%/35%/40% for 1-5 children, not less than the 5-child amount for 6 or more).

Net resources above $11,700/month are capped at that amount for the guideline calculation — courts have discretion to order more above the cap based on the child's proven needs, but it is not automatic.

example.scenario: Example: a parent with $4,000/month in net resources and 2 children.
calculation: Net resources: $4,000/month (below the $11,700 cap, so no cap adjustment applies).
2 children → standard schedule percentage: 25%.
$4,000 × 0.25 = $1,000/month guideline child support.

faq_extra.q: Does Texas child support change with 50/50 custody?
faq_extra.a: Texas uses a percentage-of-income model based only on the paying parent's net resources and number of children — it has no shared-parenting or overnight-based offset formula like income-shares states. Even under a 50/50 possession schedule, the parent not designated as primary conservator still owes the standard guideline amount; a court can deviate downward for extensive time-sharing, but there is no automatic reduction.

faq_extra.q: Is Texas child support based on gross or net income?
faq_extra.a: Net monthly resources — not gross pay and not your personal take-home after all your own expenses. It's a specific statutory calculation: gross income minus Social Security taxes, federal income tax (computed as a single filer claiming one exemption, regardless of actual filing status), union dues, and any health/dental insurance premiums ordered for the child.

faq_extra.q: What happens if my income is above the $11,700 cap?
faq_extra.a: The $11,700/month net resources cap (effective 2025-09-01) is where the guideline percentage calculation stops — courts don't automatically keep applying the percentage to income above it. A parent can still be ordered to pay more than the capped guideline amount, but only if the child's proven needs justify it, and that's a discretionary finding, not an automatic formula extension.

source.agency_name: New York State Child Support Standards Act (CSSA)
source.statute_ref: N.Y. Dom. Rel. Law § 240; Family Court Act § 413

steps: Combine both parents' income to get combined parental income.

Apply the statutory percentage based on number of children (17% for 1 child, 25% for 2, 29% for 3, 31% for 4, 35% for 5 or more) to combined income up to the cap ($193,000/year, effective 2026-03-01).
The parent with fewer overnights (even close to 50/50) pays their pro-rata share of that amount — New York does not use a shared-parenting offset formula.
Health insurance, work/education-related childcare, and unreimbursed medical costs are mandatory add-ons split pro rata by income share, on top of the basic obligation.
Above the income cap, courts apply 10 statutory factors rather than the automatic percentage.

example.scenario: Example: combined parental income of $6,000/month, 2 children, Parent A earns $4,000/mo (67% share) and Parent B earns $2,000/mo (33% share) and has fewer overnights.
calculation: Combined income: $6,000/month (below the $16,083/month cap).
2 children → statutory percentage: 25%.
$6,000 × 0.25 = $1,500/month basic obligation.
Parent A's share (67%) of $1,500 = $1,000/month guideline child support paid by Parent A.

faq_extra.q: Does New York reduce child support for shared/50-50 custody?
faq_extra.a: No. New York's CSSA formula does not include a shared-parenting or overnight-based offset like income-shares states use. Even in a near-50/50 schedule, whichever parent has fewer overnights (as designated by the court) still pays their full pro-rata share of the statutory percentage — there's no built-in credit for shared time, though a court retains discretion to deviate.

faq_extra.q: What counts as income under the New York CSSA?
faq_extra.a: Combined parental income for CSSA purposes starts from gross income and is adjusted for statutory items such as FICA/Medicare taxes, NYC or Yonkers income tax if applicable, and maintenance (spousal support) actually paid to the other parent. It is not simply take-home pay.

faq_extra.q: What happens if combined income is above $193,000/year?
faq_extra.a: Above the $193,000/year combined income cap (effective 2026-03-01), courts stop applying the automatic statutory percentage. Instead, they weigh 10 factors set out in the CSSA — including the standard of living the child would have had, the non-monied parent's needs, and the parties' financial resources — to decide support on the portion above the cap.

source.agency_name: Arizona Child Support Guidelines (Arizona Supreme Court)

steps: Determine each parent's Child Support Income (gross income from all sources).
Combine both parents' Adjusted Child Support Income to get Combined Adjusted Child Support Income.
Look up the Basic Child Support Obligation from the official Schedule of Basic Support Obligations for that combined income and number of children.
Each parent's proportionate share equals their income divided by the combined income, applied to the Combined Child Support Obligation.

Apply the Parenting Time Table adjustment: the number of parenting-time days for the paying parent maps to an adjustment percentage (0% at 0-19 days up to 50% at 164+ days), multiplied by the Basic Child Support Obligation and subtracted from that parent's share.
Apply the Self-Support Reserve Test: if the result would leave the paying parent below $1,685/month, the court may reduce the order.

example.scenario: Example: combined income $8,000/month, 2 children, Parent A earns $5,000/mo (62.5% share) and has 110 parenting-time days/year, Parent B earns $3,000/mo (37.5% share).
calculation: Basic Child Support Obligation for $8,000 combined, 2 children: $1,622/month.
Parent A's proportionate share: 62.5% × $1,622 = $1,014.
110 days falls in the 100-114 day bracket → 17.5% adjustment: $1,622 × 0.175 = $284.
$1,014 − $284 = $730/month guideline child support paid by Parent A.

faq_extra.q: How does Arizona's Parenting Time Table work?
faq_extra.a: Arizona's Parenting Time Table maps the paying parent's annual parenting-time days directly to an adjustment percentage — 0% at 0-19 days, rising in steps up to 50% at 164 or more days per year. That percentage is multiplied by the Basic Child Support Obligation and subtracted from the paying parent's proportionate share, so more parenting time directly lowers the guideline amount.

faq_extra.q: What counts as Child Support Income in Arizona?
faq_extra.a: Child Support Income in Arizona is gross income from all sources — wages, self-employment, bonuses, and other resources — before taxes, not net take-home pay. Both parents' Child Support Income is combined to look up the Basic Child Support Obligation on the official schedule.

faq_extra.q: What happens if combined income is above $30,000/month?
faq_extra.a: The official Arizona schedule of Basic Support Obligations is built out through $30,000/month in combined Child Support Income. Above that ceiling, the schedule doesn't provide a table value, so courts use discretion to set an appropriate amount rather than an automatic formula extension.

source.agency_name: Michigan Child Support Formula Manual (State Court Administrative Office)

steps: Determine each parent's net income (gross income minus taxes, mandatory retirement, and other allowable deductions).
If a parent's net income is at or below the Low Income Threshold ($1,255/month), that parent's base support obligation is simply 10% of their income.

Otherwise, combine both parents' net incomes into family income, look up the General Care Support Table bracket for that family income and number of children, and apply the General Care Equation: Base Support + (Marginal Percentage × amount over the bracket threshold), multiplied by that parent's income share.

Apply the Parental Time Offset Equation using each parent's approximate annual overnights, which produces the final monthly transfer amount directly — a negative result means Parent A pays, positive means Parent B pays.

example.scenario: Example: Parent A net income $4,500/month (60% share), Parent B net income $3,000/month (40% share), 2 children, Parent A has 100 overnights/year, Parent B has 265.
calculation: Parent A's base obligation: $2,156.32 × 60% = $1,293.79.
Parent B's base obligation: $2,156.32 × 40% = $862.53.
Parental Time Offset: (100^2.5 × 862.53 − 265^2.5 × 1293.79) / (100^2.5 + 265^2.5) — a negative result, so Parent A pays the guideline amount.

faq_extra.q: How does Michigan's Parental Time Offset work?
faq_extra.a: Michigan's Parental Time Offset Equation takes each parent's base support obligation (from the General Care Support Table) and each parent's approximate annual overnights, and computes a single net transfer amount directly — it isn't a simple percentage credit. A negative result means Parent A pays; a positive result means Parent B pays. More overnights for the paying parent reduces what they owe, but through this specific equation rather than a flat percentage table.

faq_extra.q: What is Michigan's Low Income Threshold?
faq_extra.a: Michigan's Low Income Threshold is $1,255/month in net income (2025 MCSF). A parent at or below that threshold has their base support obligation set at simply 10% of their income, instead of being run through the General Care Support Table — a simplified calculation meant to avoid pushing very low earners below subsistence.

faq_extra.q: What counts as net income in Michigan?
faq_extra.a: Net income under the Michigan Child Support Formula is gross income minus taxes, mandatory retirement contributions, and other allowable deductions specifically defined in the MCSF Manual — not just whatever shows up as take-home pay on a paystub, since some deductions employers apply (like voluntary retirement or optional benefits) aren't allowed reductions under the formula.

source.agency_name: Louisiana Revised Statutes (Legislature)

steps: Combine both parents' adjusted gross income (gross income minus preexisting child support or spousal support paid) to get combined adjusted gross income.
Look up the basic child support obligation from the official Schedule of Basic Child Support Obligations for that combined income and number of children (R.S. 9:315.19).
Each parent's share equals their income divided by the combined income, applied to the basic obligation.
Net childcare costs, health insurance premiums, and extraordinary medical/other extraordinary expenses are added to the basic obligation before proration (R.S. 9:315.8(A)).
Courts have discretion (not an automatic formula) to grant a credit when a parent exercises more than 73 days/year of physical custody.
Below $1,050/month or above $50,000/month combined income, courts determine support case-by-case.

example.scenario: Example: combined adjusted gross income $3,000/month, 2 children, Parent A earns $1,800/mo (60% share), Parent B earns $1,200/mo (40% share) and has primary custody.
calculation: Basic child support obligation for $3,000 combined, 2 children: $894/month.
Parent A's share: 60% × $894 = $536/month guideline child support paid by Parent A.

faq_extra.q: Does Louisiana reduce child support for shared custody?
faq_extra.a: Not automatically. Louisiana courts have discretion — not an automatic formula credit — to grant an adjustment when a parent exercises more than 73 days per year of physical custody. Unlike states with a graduated overnight table, Louisiana's statute leaves the exact amount of any such credit to the court's judgment.

faq_extra.q: What counts as adjusted gross income in Louisiana?
faq_extra.a: Adjusted gross income in Louisiana is each parent's gross income minus any preexisting child support or spousal support they actually pay for other cases — not simply their paycheck gross. Both parents' adjusted gross incomes are then combined to look up the basic obligation on the official schedule.

faq_extra.q: What happens if combined income is above $50,000/month?
faq_extra.a: Louisiana's official schedule covers combined adjusted gross income from $1,050 up to $50,000/month. Below $1,050 or above $50,000, the schedule doesn't provide a table value, and courts determine support case-by-case rather than extrapolating the formula automatically.

source.agency_name: Indiana Child Support Rules and Guidelines (Indiana Supreme Court)

steps: Determine each parent's weekly gross income (wages, self-employment, and other sources), then compute weekly adjusted income after allowed deductions.
Combine both parents' weekly adjusted incomes to get combined weekly adjusted income.
Look up the basic child support obligation from the official Guideline Schedules for Weekly Support Payments for that combined income and number of children (up to 8; above 8, courts use discretion).
Each parent's share equals their income divided by the combined income, applied to the basic obligation.
Above $9,200/week combined income, a fixed percentage of the excess (8.1% for 1 child up to 20.4% for 8 children) is added to the top-bracket amount.

example.scenario: Example: combined weekly adjusted income $700/week, 2 children, Parent A earns $400/wk (57% share), Parent B earns $300/wk (43% share) and has primary custody.
calculation: Basic weekly obligation for $700 combined, 2 children: $184/week.
Parent A's share: 57% × $184 ≈ $105/week guideline child support paid by Parent A.

faq_extra.q: Is Indiana child support based on weekly or monthly income?
faq_extra.a: Weekly. Indiana's guidelines are built around weekly gross income and a weekly schedule of support amounts — not monthly figures like most other states. If your pay is monthly or biweekly, it needs to be converted to a weekly figure before applying the guideline schedule.

faq_extra.q: What happens if combined income is above $9,200/week?
faq_extra.a: Above $9,200/week in combined adjusted income, Indiana adds a fixed percentage of the excess directly on top of the top-bracket schedule amount — ranging from 8.1% for one child up to 20.4% for eight children. This is an automatic formula step, not a discretionary court decision, unlike the case-by-case approach some other states use above their income caps.

faq_extra.q: Does Indiana have a parenting time credit?
faq_extra.a: Indiana's official guidelines do include a Parenting Time Credit Table that reduces the paying parent's obligation starting around 52 overnights per year. This calculator does not yet model that adjustment — treat its estimate as the pre-credit guideline amount, and expect a court-ordered figure to run lower if the paying parent has substantial parenting time.

source.agency_name: Florida Statutes (Florida Senate)

steps: Determine each parent's net monthly income (gross income minus taxes, mandatory retirement, and other allowable deductions).
Combine both parents' net incomes to get combined monthly net income.
Look up the minimum child support obligation from the official schedule for that combined income and number of children (Fla. Stat. § 61.30(6)).
Above $10,000 combined net income, add a fixed percentage of the excess (5.0% for 1 child up to 12.5% for 6 children) to the $10,000-bracket amount.
Each parent's percentage share equals their net income divided by combined net income.
Child care costs and health insurance/uncovered medical costs are added to the basic obligation before proration.
If the paying parent has more than 73 overnights/year (20%+ timesharing), a different 'gross-up' worksheet applies instead of straight proration.

example.scenario: Example: combined net income $3,000/month, 2 children, Parent A earns $1,800/mo (60% share), Parent B earns $1,200/mo (40% share) and has primary custody (under 73 overnights/year for Parent A).
calculation: Minimum child support obligation for $3,000 combined, 2 children: $1,001/month.
Parent A's share: 60% × $1,001 = $601/month guideline child support paid by Parent A.

faq_extra.q: Does Florida child support change with 50/50 or substantial timesharing?
faq_extra.a: Yes — timesharing changes the calculation method entirely rather than adjusting a percentage. If the paying parent has more than 73 overnights per year (roughly 20%+ timesharing), Florida requires a different 'gross-up' worksheet instead of the standard proration used for primary-custody arrangements, which generally produces a lower support amount.

faq_extra.q: Is Florida child support based on gross or net income?
faq_extra.a: Net monthly income — gross income minus taxes, mandatory union dues, mandatory retirement, and other allowable deductions under Fla. Stat. § 61.30. Both parents' net incomes are combined to look up the obligation on the official schedule.

faq_extra.q: What happens if combined income is above $10,000/month?
faq_extra.a: Above $10,000/month in combined net income, Florida automatically adds a fixed percentage of the excess on top of the $10,000-bracket schedule amount — ranging from 5.0% for one child up to 12.5% for six children. This is a formula step built into the statute, not a discretionary court decision.

source.agency_name: Alabama Rules of Judicial Administration, Rule 32 (Alabama Supreme Court)

steps: Combine both parents' gross monthly incomes to get combined adjusted gross income.

Look up the basic child support obligation from the official Schedule of Basic Child-Support Obligations for that combined income and number of children (below $800/month combined, a flat $50 minimum applies).
Each parent's share equals their income divided by the combined income, applied to the basic obligation.
If a parent has roughly 143-164+ overnights/year, a separate Shared Physical Custody worksheet applies instead of straight proration.
Above $20,000/month combined income, courts determine support case-by-case.

example.scenario: Example: combined gross income $3,000/month, 2 children, Parent A earns $1,800/mo (60% share), Parent B earns $1,200/mo (40% share) and has primary custody.
calculation: Basic child support obligation for $3,000 combined, 2 children: $837/month.
Parent A's share: 60% × $837 = $502/month guideline child support paid by Parent A.

faq_extra.q: Does Alabama reduce child support for shared custody?
faq_extra.a: Only through a separate track, not an automatic reduction. If a parent has roughly 143 to 164 or more overnights per year, Alabama requires a distinct Shared Physical Custody worksheet instead of straight proration of the basic obligation. The exact mechanics of that worksheet's adjustment weren't independently re-verified in this project's sourcing pass, so treat this calculator's standard estimate as a starting point if your case is near that overnight threshold.

faq_extra.q: What is Alabama's self-support reserve?
faq_extra.a: Alabama's self-support reserve is $981/month. It functions as a floor — Rule 32 guidelines are designed so a paying parent's own income generally isn't reduced below this level by a child support order, similar in purpose to the self-support reserves used in several other income-shares states.

faq_extra.q: What happens if combined income is above $20,000/month?
faq_extra.a: Alabama's official schedule extends to $20,000/month in combined gross income. Above that, the guidelines don't specify a table value, and courts determine the appropriate amount case-by-case rather than extrapolating the percentage automatically.

source.agency_name: Colorado Revised Statutes (via HB 25-1159, 2025 session)

steps: Combine both parents' adjusted gross incomes to get combined adjusted gross income.
Look up the basic child support obligation from the official Schedule of Basic Child Support Obligations for that combined income and number of children.
Each parent's share equals their income divided by the combined income, applied to the basic obligation.

Apply the Parenting Time Table: each parent's annual overnights map to a graduated credit percentage (0% at 0 overnights up to 100% at 365), replacing the old 92-overnight cliff under HB 25-1159 (effective 2026-03-01).
If the paying parent's income is at or below the self-support reserve (state minimum wage x 29 hrs/week x 50 weeks/year / 12), a reduced low-income adjustment may apply instead.
Above $40,000/month combined income, courts use discretion but the amount must not be less than the $40,000-bracket amount.

example.scenario: Example: combined income $6,000/month, 2 children, Parent A earns $3,600/mo (60% share) and has 110 overnights/year, Parent B earns $2,400/mo (40% share).
calculation: Basic child support obligation for $6,000 combined (interpolated near $5,850-$6,800 bracket), 2 children: ≈$1,600/month.
Parent A's share: 60% × $1,600 ≈ $960/month.
Parenting Time credit at 110 overnights: 18.41% × $1,600 ≈ $295 subtracted from Parent A's share.
Parent A pays approximately $665/month guideline child support.

faq_extra.q: How does Colorado's new Parenting Time Table work (HB 25-1159)?
faq_extra.a: Colorado replaced its old 92-overnight cliff with a graduated Parenting Time Table under HB 25-1159, effective 2026-03-01. Annual overnights now map to a smoothly increasing credit percentage — 0% at 0 overnights up to 100% at 365 — so every additional overnight incrementally lowers the paying parent's obligation, instead of only qualifying for a credit once you crossed a fixed day-count.

faq_extra.q: What is Colorado's self-support reserve?
faq_extra.a: Colorado's self-support reserve is calculated from the state minimum wage: hourly minimum wage × 29 hours/week × 50 weeks/year ÷ 12 months, which comes to $1,832/month using the 2026 Colorado minimum wage of $15.16/hour. If the paying parent's income falls at or below this level, a reduced low-income adjustment may apply instead of the standard formula.

faq_extra.q: What happens if combined income is above $40,000/month?
faq_extra.a: Colorado's official schedule extends to $40,000/month in combined gross income. Above that, courts use discretion to determine the appropriate amount, but the order cannot be set lower than the $40,000-bracket amount — the schedule acts as a floor rather than a hard ceiling above that point.

source.agency_name: Illinois Department of Healthcare and Family Services (HFS)

steps: Determine each parent's net income (gross income minus the standardized tax amount from the official Gross-to-Net Income Conversion Table).
Combine both parents' net incomes to get combined net income.
Look up the basic child support obligation from the official Schedule of Basic Obligations for that combined income and number of children.
Each parent's share equals their income divided by the combined income, applied to the basic obligation.
If each parent has at least 146 overnights/year, a Shared Physical Care worksheet applies instead of straight proration (threshold drops to 110 overnights starting 2027-01-01).
Above $27,375/month combined net income, courts determine support case-by-case.

example.scenario: Example: combined net income $3,000/month, 2 children, Parent A earns $1,800/mo (60% share), Parent B earns $1,200/mo (40% share) and has primary custody.
calculation: Basic child support obligation for $3,000 combined, 2 children: $1,070/month.
Parent A's share: 60% × $1,070 = $642/month guideline child support paid by Parent A.

faq_extra.q: Does Illinois reduce child support for shared parenting time?
faq_extra.a: Only if both parents clear a specific overnight threshold — it isn't a sliding scale. Illinois requires each parent to have at least 146 overnights per year (dropping to 110 starting 2027-01-01) before a Shared Physical Care worksheet replaces the standard proration calculation. Below that threshold for either parent, the standard formula applies regardless of how time is actually split.

faq_extra.q: Is Illinois child support based on gross or net income?
faq_extra.a: Net income in Illinois is not your literal take-home pay — it's gross income minus a standardized tax amount pulled from the state's official Gross-to-Net Income Conversion Table, based on income level and filing status assumptions, rather than each parent's actual individual tax situation.

faq_extra.q: What happens if combined income is above $27,375/month?
faq_extra.a: Above $27,375/month in combined net income, Illinois's official schedule doesn't provide a table value, and courts determine support case-by-case rather than automatically extending the formula.

source.agency_name: California Family Code (via leginfo.legislature.ca.gov)

steps: Determine each parent's net monthly disposable income (gross income minus taxes, mandatory union dues, mandatory retirement contributions, health premiums, and support paid for other relationships).
Combine both parents' net disposable income (TN) and identify the higher earner's income (HN) and their approximate timeshare percentage (H%).

Compute K: (1+H% if H% is 50% or less, otherwise 2-H%) multiplied by a fraction that depends on TN — 0.165+TN/82,857 up to $2,900, 0.131+TN/42,149 from $2,901-$5,000, a flat 0.250 from $5,001-$10,000, 0.10+1,499/TN from $10,001-$15,000, and 0.12+1,200/TN above $15,000.
Compute the 1-child support amount: CS = K × (HN − H% × TN).
Multiply by the statutory multiplier for the actual number of children (1.6 for 2 children, up to 2.86 for 10).
A positive result means the higher earner pays; a negative result means the lower earner pays the absolute value.

example.scenario: Example: Parent A (higher earner) net income $5,000/month, Parent B net income $3,000/month, 2 children, Parent A has 20% timeshare.
calculation: TN falls in the $5,001-$10,000 bracket → fraction = 0.250.
2-child multiplier ×1.6 = $1,632/month guideline child support paid by Parent A.

faq_extra.q: What is the California K-factor child support formula?
faq_extra.a: California's guideline uses the algebraic formula CS = K × (HN − H% × TN), where TN is both parents' combined net disposable income, HN is the higher earner's net income, H% is their approximate percentage of time with the children, and K is a fraction that itself depends on TN (ranging roughly from 0.13 to 0.25+ depending on income level). The result is then multiplied by a statutory multiplier based on the number of children (1.6 for two children, up to 2.86 for ten).

faq_extra.q: Is California child support based on gross or net income?
faq_extra.a: Net disposable income — a specific statutory figure, not gross pay or simple take-home pay. It's gross income minus state and federal taxes, mandatory union dues, mandatory retirement contributions, health insurance premiums, and support actually paid for other relationships.

faq_extra.q: How does timeshare percentage affect California child support?
faq_extra.a: Timeshare percentage (H%) enters the formula twice: first inside the K fraction itself — K uses (1 + H%) if the higher earner has 50% or less of the time, or (2 − H%) if they have more — and second directly in the base calculation (HN − H% × TN). More time with the higher earner generally reduces the support amount owed, since it changes both the K value and the income figure being multiplied.

source.agency_name: Georgia General Assembly — SB 454 (2026 session), amending O.C.G.A. § 19-6-15

params.high_income_note: Above $40,000/month combined adjusted gross income, courts set support case-by-case rather than extending the schedule formula (raised from the prior $30,000 cap effective 2024-07-01).

steps: Determine each parent's monthly gross income, then combine both for total combined adjusted gross income.
Look up the basic child support obligation from Georgia's official Schedule of Basic Obligations for that combined income and number of children.
Each parent's pro rata share equals their income divided by combined income, applied to the basic obligation.

If there is a court order for parenting time, apply the parenting time adjustment: each parent's court-ordered days with the child are raised to the power of 2.5 and cross-multiplied against the other parent's dollar share of the basic obligation — this can reduce the noncustodial parent's share to zero, or in some cases make the parent with more income owe more than the parent with less.
Add each parent's pro rata share of work-related child care costs and health insurance premiums (Schedule D) to get the presumptive child support amount.
Above $40,000/month combined adjusted gross income, courts determine support case-by-case rather than extending the table.

example.scenario: Example: combined adjusted gross income $5,000/month, 2 children, Parent A earns $3,000/mo (60% share), Parent B earns $2,000/mo (40% share) and is the noncustodial parent with 100 court-ordered parenting days/year (Parent B has 265).
calculation: Basic child support obligation for $5,000 combined, 2 children: $1,341/month.
Parent B (noncustodial)'s pro rata share before parenting time adjustment: 40% × $1,341 = $536.40/month.
Parenting time adjustment (100^2.5 vs 265^2.5 cross-multiplied against each parent's dollar share) reduces this further because Parent B has meaningful court-ordered time with the child.

Parenting time adjustment formula result: noncustodial parent's final guideline obligation is $108/month (down from the $536.40 pre-adjustment share, because Parent B has 100 court-ordered parenting days out of 365).

faq_extra.q: What changed in Georgia child support law in 2026?
faq_extra.a: Senate Bill 454 replaced the old discretionary parenting-time deviation with a mandatory formula: each parent's court-ordered days with the child are raised to the power of 2.5 and cross-multiplied against the other parent's dollar share of the basic obligation (O.C.G.A. § 19-6-15(g), effective 2026-01-01). It also raised the income cap for the schedule table from $30,000 to $40,000/month combined adjusted gross income (effective 2024-07-01) and added a new low-income adjustment mechanism and table (effective 2026-01-01).

faq_extra.q: Does Georgia reduce child support for parenting time?
faq_extra.a: Yes, but only when there is a court order awarding parenting time — without one, the court determines support without this adjustment. When a court order exists, Georgia uses a mandatory power-of-2.5 formula rather than a simple overnight-credit table like some other states use; the adjustment can reduce the noncustodial parent's obligation to zero, or in some cases require the parent with more income to pay more than the parent with less.

faq_extra.q: What happens if combined income is above $40,000/month in Georgia?
faq_extra.a: Above $40,000/month in combined adjusted gross income, Georgia's official schedule doesn't provide a table value, and courts determine support case-by-case rather than automatically extending the formula. This cap was raised from $30,000/month effective 2024-07-01.

faq_extra.q: Is there a low-income adjustment in Georgia child support?
faq_extra.a: Yes. Effective 2026-01-01, Georgia added a low-income adjustment for parents with $3,950/month or less in individual adjusted gross income, applying a sliding percentage (19% for one child up to 28% for six children) of that parent's income, phased out as income rises within that range.

source.agency_name: North Carolina Conference of Chief District Court Judges (AOC-A-162)

params.high_income_note: Above $40,000/month combined adjusted gross income, courts set support to meet the child's reasonable needs under G.S. 50-13.4(c) rather than extending the schedule.

steps: Determine each parent's monthly adjusted gross income, then combine both for total combined adjusted gross income.
If the paying parent's own adjusted gross income is below $1,150/month, a flat $50/month minimum order applies (absent deviation).

If the paying parent's income is above $1,150/month but within the self-support reserve "shaded" zone for the number of children ($1,750 for 1 child up to $3,450 for 6 children), the obligation is looked up using the paying parent's own income alone — not combined income — with no childcare or health insurance add-ons.
Otherwise, look up the basic child support obligation from the official Schedule of Basic Support Obligations using combined adjusted gross income and number of children.
Each parent's share equals their income divided by combined income, applied to the basic obligation plus reasonable work-related child care costs and health insurance premiums.

This models Worksheet A only (one parent has primary physical custody, defined as 243+ nights/year). Worksheet B (shared custody, both parents 123+ nights/year) and Worksheet C (split custody) use different calculations not modeled here.
Above $40,000/month combined adjusted gross income, courts determine support case-by-case rather than extending the schedule.

example.scenario: Example: combined adjusted gross income $7,000/month, 2 children, Parent A earns $4,000/mo (57% share), Parent B earns $3,000/mo (43% share, above the self-support reserve zone for this state) and is the noncustodial parent.
calculation: Basic child support obligation for $7,000 combined, 2 children (official schedule): $1,614/month.
Parent B's share: 42.9% × $1,614 ≈ $692/month guideline child support paid by Parent B.

faq_extra.q: What is North Carolina's self-support reserve, and how does it affect low-income parents?
faq_extra.a: North Carolina builds a self-support reserve into its child support schedule, based on the 2022 federal poverty level for one person ($1,133/month). If the paying parent's own adjusted gross income is below $1,150/month, a flat $50/month minimum order applies absent a deviation. Between $1,150/month and a higher ceiling that varies by number of children ($1,750 for one child up to $3,450 for six children), the obligation is calculated from the paying parent's income alone rather than combined income, and childcare/health insurance costs are not added on top — a mechanic most online calculators skip entirely.

faq_extra.q: What is the difference between Worksheet A, B, and C in North Carolina?
faq_extra.a: Worksheet A applies when one parent (or a third party) has primary physical custody of all the children — defined as 243+ nights per year. Worksheet B applies when parents share custody (each has the child at least 123 nights per year) of all children, or share custody of some while one has primary custody of others; the combined basic obligation is increased 50% before being prorated. Worksheet C applies when primary custody of two or more children is split between the parents. This calculator models Worksheet A only.

faq_extra.q: What happens if combined income is above $40,000/month in North Carolina?
faq_extra.a: Above $40,000/month in combined adjusted gross income, North Carolina's official schedule has no table value. Courts set support to meet the child's reasonable needs for health, education, and maintenance under G.S. 50-13.4(c), using the schedule only as a possible reference point for a minimal level of support.

source.agency_name: Ohio Department of Job and Family Services (JFS 07767, Rev. 6/2023)

params.high_income_note: Above $336,000/year combined gross income, Ohio's schedule has no table value and courts set support case-by-case.

steps: Determine each parent's annual gross income (Ohio's worksheets and schedule run on ANNUAL figures, not monthly).
Combine both parents' annual gross income for combined annual income, and calculate each parent's income share percentage.

Look up the basic obligation two ways: (a) using each parent's own individual income alone, and (b) using combined income multiplied by that parent's income share — the LOWER of the two is that parent's obligation. This is what produces the schedule's built-in self-support reserve protection for low and low-middle income parents.
A flat 10% credit applies to the paying parent's obligation if there is any court-ordered parenting time at all — Ohio does not use a graduated overnight scale like most other states.
The result is floored at the statutory minimum order of $960/year ($80/month).
Add each parent's pro rata share of work-related child care costs and health insurance premiums, then divide the annual total by 12 for the monthly order amount.
A separate cash medical support order ($388.70/child/year as of the 2023 schedule) and a 7+ children obligation multiplier table exist but are not computed by this calculator.
Above $336,000/year combined gross income, courts determine support case-by-case rather than extending the schedule.

example.scenario: Example: Parent A earns $60,000/yr, Parent B earns $40,000/yr (combined $100,000/yr), 2 children, Parent B is the noncustodial parent with 30 court-ordered parenting days/year.
calculation: Individual-income lookup for Parent B alone ($40,000/yr, 2 children): $6,926/yr.
Combined-income lookup at $100,000/yr, 2 children: $19,724/yr; Parent B's 40% income share: $7,890/yr.

Ohio takes the LOWER of the two ($6,926/yr individual lookup), then applies the flat 10% parenting-time credit (since Parent B has some court-ordered time): $6,926 × 0.90 = $6,233/yr ÷ 12 ≈ $519/month.

faq_extra.q: Why does Ohio calculate child support two different ways and take the lower amount?
faq_extra.a: Ohio's worksheet (JFS 07768) computes each parent's obligation both from their own individual income alone and from their share of the combined-income schedule amount, then uses whichever is lower. This dual calculation — not a simple income threshold — is what builds Ohio's self-support reserve protection into the schedule, and it explains why the printed schedule shows identical dollar figures across multiple child-count columns at lower income levels.

faq_extra.q: Does Ohio reduce child support for parenting time?
faq_extra.a: Yes, but as a flat 10% credit if the paying parent has ANY amount of court-ordered parenting time at all — not a graduated scale based on the number of overnights, which is how most other states (Arizona, Colorado, Georgia) handle it. More parenting time beyond having a court order doesn't increase the credit under Ohio's guidelines.

faq_extra.q: Is Ohio child support based on annual or monthly income?
faq_extra.a: Annual. Ohio's Basic Child Support Schedule (JFS 07767) and worksheets run on annual gross income figures in $600 increments, from $8,400 to $336,000/year — the final child support order is then divided by 12 to get the monthly payment amount.

faq_extra.q: What happens if combined income is above $336,000/year in Ohio?
faq_extra.a: Above $336,000/year in combined gross income, Ohio's official schedule doesn't provide a table value, and courts determine support case-by-case rather than automatically extending the formula.

source.agency_name: Pennsylvania Rules of Civil Procedure (Pa.R.Civ.P. 1910.16-3)

params.high_income_note: Above $30,000/month combined net income, Pennsylvania uses the high-income formula under Pa.R.Civ.P. 1910.16-3.1 instead of the schedule.

steps: Determine each parent's monthly net income, then combine both for combined monthly net income.

Look up the basic obligation two ways: (a) using the paying parent's own individual net income alone, and (b) using combined income multiplied by that parent's income share — the LESSER of the two applies. This mirrors the schedule's Self-Support Reserve (SSR) protection.
If the paying parent's net income is at or below the SSR ($1,255/month), courts have full discretion rather than an automatic formula result.

If the paying parent has 146 or more overnights per year (40%+ shared physical custody), a separate shared-custody adjustment formula applies (Pa.R.Civ.P. 1910.16-4(a)(1)(Part D)) — not computed by this calculator.
Add each parent's pro rata share of childcare costs and health insurance premiums.
Above $30,000/month combined net income, courts use the high-income formula (Pa.R.Civ.P. 1910.16-3.1) rather than the schedule.

example.scenario: Example: combined net income $5,000/month, 2 children, Parent A earns $3,000/mo (60% share), Parent B earns $2,000/mo (40% share) and is the noncustodial parent with under 146 overnights/year.
calculation: Individual-income lookup for Parent B alone ($2,000/mo, 2 children): $678/month.
Combined-income lookup at $5,000/mo, 2 children: $1,629/month; Parent B's 40% share: $652/month.
Pennsylvania uses the LESSER of the two: $652/month guideline child support paid by Parent B.

faq_extra.q: Why does Pennsylvania calculate child support two different ways and use the lower amount?
faq_extra.a: Pennsylvania's rule (Pa.R.Civ.P. 1910.16-2(e)(1)(ii)) requires comparing the obligation calculated from the paying parent's own income alone against the obligation calculated from combined income and that parent's income share, then using whichever is LOWER. This protects lower-income paying parents and is what produces the Self-Support Reserve built into the schedule's low end.

faq_extra.q: What is Pennsylvania's Self-Support Reserve?
faq_extra.a: The Self-Support Reserve (SSR) is $1,255/month as of the 2026 schedule — the minimum monthly net income the law reserves to the paying parent to meet basic needs. If the paying parent's net income is at or below the SSR, courts have full discretion to set support after considering actual financial resources and living expenses, rather than applying the schedule automatically.

faq_extra.q: Does shared custody reduce child support in Pennsylvania?
faq_extra.a: Yes, but only past a specific threshold: if the paying parent has 40% or more of the annual overnights (146+ nights), a separate shared-custody adjustment formula under Pa.R.Civ.P. 1910.16-4(a)(1)(Part D) applies instead of the basic calculation. Below that threshold, the standard formula applies regardless of how time is actually split.

faq_extra.q: What happens if combined income is above $30,000/month in Pennsylvania?
faq_extra.a: Above $30,000/month in combined net income, Pennsylvania's schedule doesn't provide a table value. Courts instead use the high-income formula under Pa.R.Civ.P. 1910.16-3.1 rather than extending the basic schedule.

source.agency_name: Code of Virginia § 20-108.2 (Virginia Legislative Information System)

params.high_income_note: Above $42,500/month combined gross income, Virginia's own formula applies directly: the $42,500 schedule amount plus a percentage of income above that (2.6%-5.0% by number of children) -- not judicial discretion.

steps: Determine each parent's monthly gross income, then combine both for combined monthly gross income.
Look up the basic child support obligation from the official Schedule of Monthly Basic Child Support Obligations for that combined income and number of children.

If the parent with fewer overnights has 90 days or fewer per year, use the standard sole-custody calculation: each parent's pro rata share of the basic obligation plus childcare and health insurance costs.

If the parent with fewer overnights has MORE than 90 days per year, the shared-custody formula applies instead: multiply the basic obligation by 1.4 (the "shared support need"), then cross-multiply each parent's custody share and income share against the other's — the difference is the shared-custody support, paid by whichever parent's computed share is larger.
The presumptive amount is the shared-custody result UNLESS the sole-custody amount is actually less, in which case the lesser amount applies.
Above $42,500/month combined gross income, Virginia's statute applies its own formula directly (the $42,500 amount plus a percentage of income above that) rather than judicial discretion.

example.scenario: Example: combined gross income $6,000/month, 2 children, Parent A earns $3,600/mo (60% share), Parent B earns $2,400/mo (40% share) and has 120 overnights/year (over the 90-day shared-custody threshold).
calculation: Basic child support obligation for $6,000 combined, 2 children: $1,489/month.
Shared support need: $1,489 × 1.4 = $2,085/month.

Cross-multiplying custody shares (Parent B 32.9%, Parent A 67.1%) and income shares against the shared support need gives $148/month — well below the sole-custody amount ($596/month), so the lesser (shared-custody) amount applies.

faq_extra.q: How does Virginia's shared custody child support formula work?
faq_extra.a: When the parent with fewer overnights has more than 90 days per year, Virginia uses a distinct shared-custody formula instead of simple proration: the basic schedule obligation is multiplied by 1.4 to get the "shared support need," which is then cross-multiplied by each parent's custody share (days with the child ÷ 365) and income share. The difference between what each parent would theoretically owe the other becomes the actual support amount — paid by whichever parent's calculated share is larger. If this shared-custody amount would be more than the standard sole-custody amount, the lesser of the two applies.

faq_extra.q: What happens if combined income is above $42,500/month in Virginia?
faq_extra.a: Unlike most states, Virginia doesn't leave amounts above the schedule cap to judicial discretion. The statute (Va. Code § 20-108.2(B)) specifies a formula directly: take the schedule amount at $42,500/month and add a percentage of income above that threshold — 2.6% for one child up to 5.0% for six children. This cap was raised from $35,000/month by SB 805, effective 2025-07-01, the first update to Virginia's schedule since 2014.

faq_extra.q: What counts as a "day" for Virginia's 90-day shared custody threshold?
faq_extra.a: A day is a 24-hour period. If the parent with fewer overnights has an overnight with the child but physical custody for less than 24 hours during that period, each parent is presumed to get credit for half a day. The 90-day threshold determines whether the shared-custody formula applies at all — below it, the standard sole-custody proration is used regardless of how time is actually split.

source.agency_name: Revised Code of Washington § 26.19.020 (Washington State Legislature)

params.high_income_note: Above $50,000/month combined net income, Washington's economic table has no further value -- courts may exceed the presumptive amount only upon written findings of fact.

steps: Determine each parent's monthly net income, then combine both for combined monthly net income.
Look up the basic child support obligation from the official Economic Table for combined income and number of children (table covers 1-5 children; 6+ uses the 5-child figure).
Each parent's share equals their income divided by combined income, applied to the basic obligation.

A presumptive minimum of $50/child/month applies whenever the paying parent's net income is below the Self-Support Reserve (180% of the one-person federal poverty guideline, $2,394/month as of the 2026 guidelines).
Above that minimum, the basic obligation may not reduce the paying parent's net income below the Self-Support Reserve.
Total support owed for ALL of that parent's children (not just this case) is capped at 45% of net income.
Above $50,000/month combined net income, courts may exceed the presumptive amount only upon written findings of fact.

example.scenario: Example: combined net income $8,500/month, 2 children, Parent A earns $5,000/mo (59% share), Parent B earns $3,500/mo (41% share, above the $2,394 Self-Support Reserve) and is the noncustodial parent.
calculation: Basic child support obligation for $8,500 combined, 2 children: $993.50/month.
Parent B's share: 41.18% × $993.50 ≈ $409/month — above both the $100/month minimum (2 × $50) and the Self-Support Reserve floor, so the standard prorated amount applies.

faq_extra.q: What is Washington's Self-Support Reserve and how does it work?
faq_extra.a: The Self-Support Reserve (SSR) is 180% of the federal poverty guideline for a one-person household — $2,394/month as of the 2026 guidelines. The basic child support obligation generally may not reduce the paying parent's net income below this amount. However, a presumptive minimum of $50/child/month always applies, even if that would dip into the reserve, unless the obligor shows it would be unjust.

faq_extra.q: Is there a cap on how much child support Washington can order?
faq_extra.a: Yes — total support a parent owes for ALL of their biological or legal children (not just the children in a specific case) cannot exceed 45% of that parent's net income, except for good cause shown (such as substantial wealth or special medical/educational needs). This calculator applies the cap to the case at hand but can't account for a parent's other support obligations outside it.

faq_extra.q: What happens if combined income is above $50,000/month in Washington?
faq_extra.a: Above $50,000/month in combined net income, Washington's Economic Table doesn't extend further — courts may exceed the presumptive amount set at $50,000 only upon written findings of fact, not automatically. This threshold itself was unchanged by the 2026 update; what changed was the LOWER threshold, which rose from $1,000 to $2,200/month.

faq_extra.q: Does Washington's schedule cover 6 or more children?
faq_extra.a: No — Washington's official Economic Table (RCW 26.19.020) only has columns for one through five children, unlike most other states' schedules which extend to six. For six or more children, this calculator uses the five-child figure as an approximation, since the statute itself doesn't define a specific amount beyond five.

source.agency_name: New Jersey Court Rules, Appendix IX-F (Administrative Office of the Courts)
source.statute_ref: N.J. Ct. R. 5:6A, Appendix IX-A through IX-F

params.high_income_note: Above $3,600/week combined net income ($187,200/year), courts apply the $3,600 guideline amount as a floor and add a discretionary amount rather than extrapolating the schedule.

steps: Determine each parent's net weekly income (New Jersey's guidelines run on weekly figures, not monthly).
Combine both parents' net weekly income for combined net weekly income.
Look up the basic child support award from the official Schedule of Child Support Awards (Appendix IX-F) for that combined income and number of children.
Each parent's share equals their income divided by combined income, applied to the basic award.

The Self-Support Reserve (150% of the one-person federal poverty guideline, $460/week as of 2026) is checked: if paying support would drop the paying parent's net income below the reserve AND the receiving parent's net income minus their share would stay above the reserve, courts review income and living expenses rather than applying the schedule automatically.
Below $180/week combined net income, and above the $3,600/week cap, the guideline is explicitly discretionary rather than a formula — the rule text says not to extrapolate the schedule.

example.scenario: Example: combined net income $1,000/week, 2 children, Parent A earns $600/wk (60% share), Parent B earns $400/wk (40% share) and is the noncustodial parent.
calculation: Basic child support award for $1,000 combined, 2 children: $339/week.
Parent B's share: 40% × $339 ≈ $136/week guideline child support paid by Parent B.

faq_extra.q: Is New Jersey child support based on weekly or monthly income?
faq_extra.a: Weekly. New Jersey's Schedule of Child Support Awards (Appendix IX-F) and worksheets run on net WEEKLY income figures, from $180 to $3,600/week combined — not monthly amounts like most other states. If your pay is monthly or biweekly, it needs to be converted to a weekly figure first.

faq_extra.q: What is New Jersey's Self-Support Reserve?
faq_extra.a: The Self-Support Reserve is 150% of the federal poverty guideline for a one-person household — $460/week as of January 1, 2026. It only adjusts the award when BOTH conditions are met: paying the guideline amount would drop the paying parent's net income below the reserve, AND the receiving parent's income minus their own share would stay above the reserve. If the receiving parent would also fall below the reserve, no adjustment is made regardless of the paying parent's income — courts prioritize the custodial household's basic needs.

faq_extra.q: What happens if combined income is above $3,600/week in New Jersey?
faq_extra.a: Above $3,600/week ($187,200/year) in combined net income, New Jersey's rule explicitly says not to extrapolate the schedule. Courts instead apply the $3,600 guideline amount as a floor and add a discretionary amount based on the family's actual circumstances under N.J.S.A. 2A:34-23, rather than a formula.

source.agency_name: Tennessee Department of Human Services, Child Support Guidelines (Tenn. Comp. R. & Regs. 1240-02-04)

params.high_income_note: Above $28,250/month combined adjusted gross income, Tennessee's statute applies its own formula directly: the $28,250 schedule amount plus a percentage of income above that (6.81%-8.66% by number of children).

steps: Determine each parent's monthly adjusted gross income, then combine both for combined monthly adjusted gross income.
Look up the basic child support obligation (BCSO) from the official Schedule of Basic Child Support Obligations for that combined income and number of children.
Each parent's pro rata share equals their income divided by combined income, applied to the BCSO.

If the paying parent (ARP) has 92 or more days of parenting time per year, a variable multiplier (days x 2/182.5) adjusts the BCSO upward, and the difference is credited against the paying parent's share to account for duplicated/transferred expenses.

That result is compared against a schedule lookup using the paying parent's own income alone (the Self-Support Reserve, $957/month) — the LESSER of the two applies, and if the individual lookup wins, the parenting-time credit is effectively forfeited.
Above $28,250/month combined adjusted gross income, Tennessee applies its own formula directly rather than judicial discretion.

example.scenario: Example (the rule's own worked example): $1,000/month BCSO, paying parent (ARP) has 94 parenting days/year, custodial parent (PRP) has a 40% income share.
calculation: Variable multiplier: 94 × 0.0109589 = 1.0301366.
Adjusted BCSO: $1,000 × 1.0301366 = $1,030.14; difference from base: $30.14.
PRP's 40% share of that difference: $12.06, credited against the ARP's 60% share ($600): $600 − $12.06 = $587.94/month, assuming this is above the Self-Support Reserve comparison.

faq_extra.q: How does Tennessee's parenting time credit work?
faq_extra.a: If the paying parent (ARP) has 92 or more days of parenting time per year, Tennessee applies a "variable multiplier" — the ARP's annual parenting days times 2/182.5 — to the basic child support obligation (BCSO). The increase this produces is prorated by the custodial parent's income share and credited against the paying parent's obligation, reflecting the duplicated and transferred expenses of shared parenting time. At exactly half the year (182.5 days), this doubles the BCSO before crediting.

faq_extra.q: What is Tennessee's Self-Support Reserve, and does it cancel the parenting time credit?
faq_extra.a: The Self-Support Reserve (SSR) is 90% of the federal poverty guideline for one person — $957/month. Tennessee compares the parenting-time-credited obligation against a schedule lookup using the paying parent's own income alone; whichever is LOWER applies. If the individual-income lookup is lower, the paying parent effectively loses the parenting-time credit, since the final amount comes from the SSR comparison instead.

faq_extra.q: What happens if combined income is above $28,250/month in Tennessee?
faq_extra.a: Above $28,250/month in combined adjusted gross income, Tennessee's rule specifies its own formula directly rather than leaving it to judicial discretion: the $28,250 schedule amount plus a percentage of the income above that threshold, ranging from 6.81% for one child up to 8.66% for five or more children.

source.agency_name: Missouri Supreme Court, Form 14 Schedule of Basic Child Support Obligations (2024 Guideline Review)

params.high_income_note: Above $40,000/month combined adjusted gross income, or beyond six children, Missouri courts consider deviation factors under Form 14 rather than an automatic formula.

steps: Determine each parent's monthly adjusted gross income, then combine both for combined monthly adjusted gross income.
Look up the basic child support amount from the official Schedule of Basic Child Support Obligations (Form 14) for that combined income and number of children.
Each parent's share equals their income divided by combined income, applied to the basic amount, plus each parent's pro rata share of childcare and health insurance costs.

A stepped overnight-visitation credit applies based on the paying parent's annual overnights — 0% below 36 nights, rising in steps to 34% at 181-183 nights — computed on the FULL basic child support amount and subtracted from the paying parent's share.

For low-income cases (the paying parent's income and children count fall in the schedule's shaded self-support-reserve area), two calculations are compared using the SAME overnight credit dollar amount: the standard proration, and a lookup at the paying parent's own income alone (100%) — the LOWER applies.
Above $40,000/month combined adjusted gross income, or beyond six children, courts consider deviation factors rather than an automatic formula.

example.scenario: Example: combined adjusted gross income $6,000/month, 2 children, Parent A earns $3,600/mo (60% share), Parent B earns $2,400/mo (40% share) and is the noncustodial parent with 100 overnights/year (10% credit bracket).
calculation: Basic child support amount for $6,000 combined, 2 children: $1,589/month.
Overnight credit: $1,589 × 10% = $159.
Parent B's share: 40% × $1,589 = $635.60, minus the $159 credit = $477/month guideline child support paid by Parent B.

faq_extra.q: How does Missouri's overnight visitation credit work?
faq_extra.a: Missouri's Form 14 applies a stepped percentage credit based on the paying parent's annual overnights with the child — 0% below 36 nights, rising through fixed brackets (6% at 36-72, 10% at 92-109, up to 34% at 181-183 nights). Unlike some states' graduated formulas, this credit is calculated on the FULL basic child support amount (not just the paying parent's share) and then subtracted from that parent's prorated obligation.

faq_extra.q: Does Missouri have a self-support reserve for low-income parents?
faq_extra.a: Yes. When the paying parent's income and the number of children fall within the schedule's shaded (low-income) area, Missouri compares two calculations using the same overnight-visitation credit dollar amount: the standard combined-income proration, and a lookup using the paying parent's own income alone. Whichever produces the lower amount is used.

faq_extra.q: What happens if combined income is above $40,000/month in Missouri, or there are more than six children?
faq_extra.a: Above $40,000/month in combined adjusted gross income, or when more than six children are involved, Missouri's Form 14 doesn't provide an automatic schedule value — courts instead consider deviation factors under Form 14's comment provisions rather than extending the formula.

source.agency_name: Wisconsin Department of Children and Families, Bureau of Child Support (Wis. Admin. Code DCF 150)

params.high_income_note: Above $7,000/month, progressively lower percentages apply to each higher income tier -- not judicial discretion.

steps: Wisconsin is NOT an income-shares state -- the Percentage Standard applies to the paying parent's OWN gross income only, never combined income.

If the paying parent has fewer than 92 overnights/year with the child (sole placement), support equals a flat percentage of that parent's gross monthly income: 17% for one child, 25% for two, 29% for three, 31% for four, 34% for five or more.
Above $7,000/month, progressively lower percentages apply to income above that threshold, then again above $12,500/month (a tiered formula, not judicial discretion).

If the paying parent has 92 or more overnights/year (at least 25% of the time, shared placement), a different formula applies: each parent's percentage-of-income amount is multiplied by 1.5, then by the share of time the child spends with the OTHER parent -- the difference between the two results is the support owed, paid by whichever parent's amount is larger.
Wisconsin also has separate low-income payer guidelines (75%-150% of the federal poverty guideline) and split-placement guidelines, not modeled by this calculator.

example.scenario: Example (the state's own worked example): 2 children, Parent A earns $2,000/mo and has 219 placement days/year (60%), Parent B earns $3,000/mo and has 146 placement days/year (40%) -- both over the 92-day shared-placement threshold.
calculation: Line 2 (×150%): Parent A: $750. Parent B: $1,125.
Line 3 (× time with the OTHER parent): Parent A: $750 × 40% = $300. Parent B: $1,125 × 60% = $675.
Offset: $675 − $300 = $375/month owed by Parent B (the parent with the larger Line 3 amount).

faq_extra.q: Is Wisconsin an income-shares state?
faq_extra.a: No. Unlike most states covered here, Wisconsin uses a Percentage of Income Standard: support is based on the paying parent's OWN gross income alone, not combined parental income. The percentages are fixed by number of children (17% for one child up to 34% for five or more) rather than looked up on an income-based schedule table.

faq_extra.q: How does Wisconsin's shared-placement formula work?
faq_extra.a: When the paying parent has 92 or more overnights per year (at least 25% of the time), Wisconsin uses a cross-formula instead of a flat percentage: each parent's percentage-of-income amount is multiplied by 1.5 (to account for both parents' basic support costs), then by the share of time the child spends with the OTHER parent. The difference between the two parents' results is the actual support amount, paid by whichever parent's calculated amount is larger.

faq_extra.q: What happens with high-income paying parents in Wisconsin?
faq_extra.a: Above $7,000/month in gross income, Wisconsin applies progressively lower percentages to each additional tier of income (14%-27% between $7,000-$12,500/month, then 10%-20% above $12,500/month, depending on the number of children) rather than applying the standard percentage to the full amount or leaving it to judicial discretion.

source.agency_name: Minnesota Statutes, Chapter 518A (Office of the Revisor of Statutes)

params.high_income_note: Above $20,000/month combined parental income, the presumptive obligation is capped at the $20,000 schedule figure -- a higher amount requires the factors in Minn. Stat. 518A.43.

steps: Determine each parent's monthly gross income (parental income for determining child support, PICS), then combine both for combined PICS.
Look up the basic support obligation from the official guideline table for that combined income and number of children.

Apply the parenting time adjustment: each parent's annual overnights are raised to the power of 3, then cross-multiplied against the other parent's dollar share of the basic obligation -- this can shift who pays and how much, not just apply a simple credit.

Apply the Self-Support Reserve: subtract 120% of the one-person federal poverty guideline from the paying parent's income. If what remains is less than the guideline amount, the obligation is reduced down to that remaining amount, floored at a flat statutory minimum ($50/month for one child up to $100/month for six or more).
Above $20,000/month combined income, the obligation is capped at the $20,000 schedule figure rather than extending further.

example.scenario: Example: combined income $5,000/month, 2 children, Parent A earns $3,000/mo (60% share), Parent B earns $2,000/mo (40% share) and is the noncustodial parent with 100 court-ordered overnights/year.
calculation: Basic support obligation for $5,000 combined, 2 children: $1,132/month.
Parent B's pre-adjustment share: 40% × $1,132 ≈ $453/month.

The power-of-3 parenting time adjustment (100^3 vs 265^3 cross-multiplied against each parent's dollar share) reduces this to $58/month -- a much sharper reduction than a power-of-2.5 formula would produce for the same days.

faq_extra.q: How does Minnesota's parenting time adjustment work?
faq_extra.a: Minnesota raises each parent's annual overnights to the POWER OF 3 (not a simple percentage credit), then cross-multiplies that against the other parent's dollar share of the basic support obligation (Minn. Stat. § 518A.36). Because the exponent is higher than similar formulas in other states (Georgia uses 2.5, for comparison), Minnesota's adjustment grows more sharply as parenting time increases. It only applies when there is a court order awarding parenting time.

faq_extra.q: What is Minnesota's Self-Support Reserve?
faq_extra.a: The Self-Support Reserve is 120% of the federal poverty guideline for a one-person household. Minnesota subtracts this from the paying parent's income to find how much is actually available for support. If that available amount is less than the guideline obligation, the order is reduced down to the available amount -- but never below a flat statutory minimum ($50/month for one child, rising to $100/month for six or more children).

faq_extra.q: What happens if combined income is above $20,000/month in Minnesota?
faq_extra.a: Above $20,000/month in combined parental income, Minnesota caps the presumptive obligation at the $20,000 schedule figure rather than extrapolating further. A higher amount can be demonstrated only through the factors in Minn. Stat. 518A.43, not an automatic formula extension.

source.agency_name: Maryland General Assembly, Md. Code, Family Law § 12-204

params.high_income_note: Above $30,000/month combined adjusted actual income, Maryland's schedule has no further value -- courts use discretion in setting support.

steps: Determine each parent's monthly adjusted actual income, then combine both for combined monthly adjusted actual income.

Look up the basic child support obligation from the official Schedule of Basic Child Support Obligations for that combined income and number of children -- the schedule's own self-support reserve adjustment is already built into its lower-income rows.
If neither parent has shared physical custody (92+ overnights/year for the parent with less time), each parent's share of the basic obligation plus childcare/health costs is the standard proration.

If both parents have shared physical custody (92+ overnights each), a cross-formula applies instead: the basic obligation is multiplied by 1.5, then each parent's share is cross-multiplied by the OTHER parent's time share -- the difference is the support owed, paid by whichever parent's theoretical amount is larger.
In the narrow 92-109 overnight band specifically, an additional graduated bonus (2%-10%, higher for fewer nights within that band) increases the paying parent's theoretical amount.
The shared-custody result is capped at the sole-custody amount if that would be lower.
Above $30,000/month combined income, courts use discretion rather than an automatic formula.

example.scenario: Example: combined adjusted actual income $5,000/month, 2 children, Parent A earns $3,000/mo (60% share), Parent B earns $2,000/mo (40% share) and has 100 overnights/year (in the narrow 92-109 bonus band).
calculation: Basic child support obligation for $5,000 combined, 2 children: $1,356/month.
Adjusted (x1.5) basic obligation for the shared-custody cross-formula: $2,034/month.

Cross-multiplying each parent's income share and time share, plus the narrow-band bonus for Parent B's 100 overnights (99-102 night bracket, +6%), produces $292/month owed by Parent B -- below the $542/month sole-custody amount, so the shared-custody result applies.

faq_extra.q: What is Maryland's narrow-band shared custody bonus?
faq_extra.a: When a parent with shared physical custody has between 92 and 109 overnights per year (25%-30% of the time), Maryland adds an EXTRA graduated bonus to that parent's theoretical obligation in the cross-formula -- 10% at 92-94 nights, stepping down to 2% at 106-109 nights. This is on top of the standard shared-custody cross-multiplication, and only applies in this specific narrow range.

faq_extra.q: Is Maryland's self-support reserve a separate calculation?
faq_extra.a: No -- unlike some states that compare two separate calculations, Maryland bakes the self-support reserve adjustment directly into the printed Schedule of Basic Child Support Obligations. Rows below roughly $1,650/month combined income (marked with an asterisk in the statute) already reflect the reduced, reserve-adjusted amount, so no extra step is needed beyond the standard schedule lookup.

faq_extra.q: What happens if combined income is above $30,000/month in Maryland?
faq_extra.a: Above $30,000/month in combined adjusted actual income, Maryland's schedule provides no further value. Courts use their discretion in setting the support amount (Md. Fam. Law § 12-204(d)) rather than extending the schedule with an automatic formula.

source.agency_name: South Carolina Department of Social Services, Child Support Guidelines (DSS Booklet 2819, Nov. 2024)

params.high_income_note: Above $40,000/month combined gross income, South Carolina courts determine awards case-by-case rather than an automatic formula.

steps: Determine each parent's monthly gross income, then combine both for combined monthly gross income.
Look up the basic child support obligation from the official Schedule of Basic Support Obligations for that combined income and number of children.
For sole custody (Worksheet A), compare the standard proration to a lookup using the paying parent's own income alone (self-support reserve, $1,010.50/month) -- the lower amount applies.

For shared physical custody (both parents more than 109 overnights/year, Worksheet C), a different formula applies: the basic obligation is multiplied by 1.5, apportioned to each parent by their OWN income share, then multiplied by the percentage of time the child spends WITH THAT SAME parent. Whichever parent's result is larger pays the difference -- unlike some states, this can make the majority-time parent the one who pays.
A graduated transition applies between 110 and 128 overnights, blending the sole-custody and shared-custody amounts -- not modeled by this calculator.
Above $40,000/month combined income, courts determine awards case-by-case rather than extending the schedule.

example.scenario: Example: combined gross income $5,000/month, 2 children, Parent A earns $3,000/mo (60% share, 60% of the child's time), Parent B earns $2,000/mo (40% share, 40% of the child's time) -- shared custody, both over the 109-overnight threshold.
calculation: Basic child support obligation for $5,000 combined, 2 children (official schedule): $1,226/month.
Shared custody obligation (x1.5): $1,839/month.
Parent A's obligation: $1,839 × 60% × 60% ≈ $662. Parent B's obligation: $1,839 × 40% × 40% ≈ $294.
Parent A's result is larger, so Parent A pays the difference: $662 − $294 ≈ $368/month.

faq_extra.q: How is South Carolina's shared custody formula different from other states?
faq_extra.a: In South Carolina's Worksheet C (both parents with more than 109 overnights/year), each parent's notional obligation is their OWN income share multiplied by their OWN time share with the child -- not the other parent's time share, which is how Virginia and Maryland compute it. Whichever parent's result is larger pays the difference. This can mean the parent with MORE time and MORE income ends up paying support, which surprises people used to the more common cross-multiplication formula.

faq_extra.q: What is South Carolina's self-support reserve?
faq_extra.a: The self-support reserve is $1,010.50/month as of the 2024 guidelines update. For sole-custody cases, South Carolina compares the standard proration against a schedule lookup using only the paying parent's own income, and uses whichever is lower -- protecting low-income parents from being reduced below a basic subsistence level.

faq_extra.q: What happens if combined income is above $40,000/month in South Carolina?
faq_extra.a: Above $40,000/month in combined gross income (raised from $30,000 by the 2024 guidelines update, the first revision since 2014), South Carolina's schedule provides no further value. Courts determine the award case-by-case rather than extrapolating the formula.

source.agency_name: Oregon Department of Justice, Child Support Program (OAR 137-050)

params.high_income_note: Above $30,000/month combined gross adjusted income, or beyond 10 children, Oregon's obligation is the same as for $30,000/month -- not extrapolated further.

steps: Determine each parent's monthly gross adjusted income, then combine both for combined monthly gross adjusted income.
Look up the basic child support obligation from the official Guidelines Scale for that combined income and number of children (up to 10).
Each parent's share equals their income divided by combined income, applied to the basic obligation.
Apply the parenting time credit: an exact logistic formula based on the paying parent's annual overnights (not a simple percentage table) reduces that parent's share.
The result may not exceed the paying parent's income available for support (income minus the $1,522/month Self-Support Reserve), and a rebuttable $100/month minimum order applies.
Above $30,000/month combined income, the obligation is the same as for $30,000/month rather than extrapolated further.

example.scenario: Example: combined gross adjusted income $5,000/month, 2 children, Parent A earns $3,000/mo (60% share), Parent B earns $2,000/mo (40% share) and is the noncustodial parent with 100 overnights/year.
calculation: Basic child support obligation for $5,000 combined, 2 children (official scale): $1,136/month.
Parent B's pre-credit share: 40% × $1,136 ≈ $454/month.
Parenting time credit at 100/365 overnights: the logistic formula gives 15.4% of the basic obligation (≈$175), reducing Parent B's share to $280/month.

faq_extra.q: How does Oregon's parenting time credit formula work?
faq_extra.a: Unlike most states, which use a lookup table for parenting-time credits, Oregon uses an exact mathematical formula: credit% = 1/(1+e^(-7.14×((overnights/365)-0.5))) - 0.0274 + (2×0.0274×(overnights/365)). This is a logistic (S-shaped) curve that produces a smooth, continuously increasing credit as overnights rise, reaching 50% at exactly equal (182.5/182.5) parenting time.

faq_extra.q: What is Oregon's Self-Support Reserve?
faq_extra.a: The Self-Support Reserve is $1,522/month as of the 2026 guidelines (based on the federal poverty guideline, multiplied by 1.167 for estimated taxes). The paying parent's total obligation may not exceed their income minus this reserve amount -- protecting low-income parents from being reduced below a basic subsistence level.

faq_extra.q: What happens if combined income is above $30,000/month in Oregon?
faq_extra.a: Above $30,000/month in combined gross adjusted income (or beyond 10 children), Oregon's guideline caps the obligation at the $30,000 schedule figure rather than extrapolating further. A newer scale with a $50,000 cap and 6 children columns has been proposed by Oregon's Guidelines Advisory Committee as of 2026 but is not yet adopted into the administrative rule.

source.agency_name: Nevada Division of Welfare and Supportive Services (Nev. Admin. Code § 425.140)

steps: Nevada is NOT an income-shares state -- the base obligation is a tiered percentage of the OBLIGOR's own gross monthly income only, never combined income.
For the first $6,000/month: 16% for one child, 22% for two, 26% for three, 28% for four, plus 2% for each additional child.
For the portion between $6,000 and $10,000/month: 8% for one child, 11% for two, 13% for three, 14% for four, plus 1% for each additional child.
For the portion above $10,000/month: 4% for one child, 6% for two, 6% for three, 7% for four, plus 0.5% for each additional child -- there is no income cap.

The obligor (paying parent) is determined by which parent has the minority of overnights; Nevada has no mandatory shared-placement cross-formula -- courts adjust for time-sharing at their discretion under NAC 425.150.

If the obligor's income is very low, courts may use a separately published low-income schedule (NAC 425.145, updated annually by the Administrative Office of the Courts) instead -- not modeled by this calculator.

example.scenario: Example (the regulation's own worked example): obligor earns $14,000/month, 2 children.
calculation: Total base child support obligation: $1,320 + $440 + $240 = $2,000/month.

faq_extra.q: Is Nevada an income-shares state?
faq_extra.a: No. Nevada's Base Child Support Obligation (NAC 425.140) is based entirely on the OBLIGOR's (paying parent's) own gross monthly income -- the other parent's income doesn't factor into the base calculation at all, unlike income-shares states such as Texas or Georgia.

faq_extra.q: Does Nevada have an income cap for child support?
faq_extra.a: No. Unlike most states, Nevada's formula has no upper income limit -- the top tier (income above $10,000/month) simply continues to apply its percentage (4%-8% depending on number of children) to all income above that threshold, however high.

faq_extra.q: Does shared custody reduce child support in Nevada?
faq_extra.a: Not automatically through a fixed formula. Nevada courts may adjust the base obligation under NAC 425.150 considering the relative income of both households and time-sharing costs, among other discretionary factors -- but there is no mandatory shared-placement cross-formula like some other states use.

source.agency_name: The Family Court of the State of Delaware (Form 509, Rules 500-509)
source.statute_ref: Del. Fam. Ct. Civ. P. R. 500-509

steps: Delaware uses the Melson Formula -- one of only 3 states (with Hawaii and Montana) that use this three-step model instead of income shares or a flat percentage.
Step 1: Each parent keeps a Self-Support Allowance ($1,600/month as of 2026, 120% of the one-person federal poverty guideline) before anything else is counted -- this comes off gross income first.

Step 2: The children's Primary Support need (a per-child allowance of $420 plus a per-household allowance of $380, plus childcare and health insurance) is prorated between the parents by their share of remaining income (Net Available Income, NAI).

Step 3: The Standard of Living Adjustment (SOLA) shares each parent's LEFTOVER income (after their own primary share) with the children, at a percentage that rises with the number of children (12% for one, 17% for two, 21% for three, +2% each additional) -- simulating what the children would enjoy if the family lived together.
A High Income Offset reduces the SOLA base by 30% of any combined income above 10 times the Self-Support Allowance ($16,000/month combined).

A parenting-time credit (10% at 80-124 overnights/year, 30% at 125-163) reduces the paying parent's obligation, and a Self-Support Protection caps the obligation at 50% of that parent's own Net Available Income.
A minimum order applies: $160/month for one child, $240/month for two or more.
